abstract:
Inspired by the recent synthesis of bipartite sodalite clathrates $X\text{B}_3\text{C}_3$ ($X = \text{Sr, La}$), we employed density functional theory (DFT) calculations to investigate this structural family, exploring configurations with up to two distinct metal guests. A DFT-chemical pressure analysis of the $X = \text{Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba}$ series demonstrates that the ionic radius of the metal guest is a decisive factor for stability; specifically, the metal size must be precisely tuned to stabilize the boron-carbon framework against ambient-pressure dynamic instabilities. 

By conducting high-throughput DFT screenings of 105 binary-guest compounds with $Pm\bar{3}$ symmetry ($XY\text{B}_6\text{C}_6$, where $X$ and $Y$ are electropositive metals), we identified 22 phases that remain dynamically stable at 1 atm. This discovery expands the library of potentially synthesizable clathrate phases by 19, including 18 new metallic conductors and one insulator. Our results show that the superconducting critical temperature ($T_c$) and the density of states at the Fermi level can be effectively modulated by adjusting the average oxidation state of the guest atoms, with $T_c$ reaching its peak at an average valence of +1.5. Among all studied $Pm\bar{3}n$ $X\text{B}_3\text{C}_3$ and $Pm\bar{3}$ $XY\text{B}_6\text{C}_6$ phases, $\text{KPbB}_6\text{C}_6$ stands out with a predicted ambient-pressure Eliashberg $T_c$ of 88 K. Thermodynamic calculations suggest that $\text{KPbB}_6\text{C}_6$ can be synthesized using high-pressure, high-temperature methods and successfully quenched to ambient conditions, offering a promising route toward high-temperature superconductivity in recoverable covalent materials.
